   103	#[derive(Default, serde::Deserialize)]
   104	#[serde(default)]
   105	pub struct SpirvInParameters {
   106	    pub adjust_coordinate_space: bool,
   107	}
   108	impl From<&SpirvInParameters> for naga::front::spv::Options {
   109	    fn from(value: &SpirvInParameters) -> Self {
   110	        Self {
   111	            adjust_coordinate_space: value.adjust_coordinate_space,
   112	            ..Default::default()
   113	        }
   114	    }
   115	}
   116	
   117	#[derive(serde::Deserialize)]
   118	#[serde(default)]
   119	pub struct SpirvOutParameters {
   120	    pub version: SpvOutVersion,
   121	    pub capabilities: naga::FastHashSet<spirv::Capability>,
   122	    pub debug: bool,
   123	    pub adjust_coordinate_space: bool,
   124	    pub force_point_size: bool,
   125	    pub clamp_frag_depth: bool,
   126	    pub separate_entry_points: bool,
   127	    #[serde(deserialize_with = "deserialize_binding_map")]
   128	    pub binding_map: naga::back::spv::BindingMap,
   129	    pub ray_query_initialization_tracking: bool,
   130	    pub use_storage_input_output_16: bool,
   131	}
   132	impl Default for SpirvOutParameters {
   133	    fn default() -> Self {
   134	        Self {
   135	            version: SpvOutVersion::default(),
   136	            capabilities: naga::FastHashSet::default(),
   137	            debug: false,
   138	            adjust_coordinate_space: false,
   139	            force_point_size: false,
   140	            clamp_frag_depth: false,
   141	            separate_entry_points: false,
   142	            ray_query_initialization_tracking: true,
   143	            use_storage_input_output_16: true,
   144	            binding_map: naga::back::spv::BindingMap::default(),
   145	        }
   146	    }
   147	}
   148	impl SpirvOutParameters {
   149	    pub fn to_options<'a>(
   150	        &'a self,
   151	        shared_info: &WriterSharedOptions,
   152	        debug_info: Option<naga::back::spv::DebugInfo<'a>>,
   153	    ) -> naga::back::spv::Options<'a> {
   154	        use naga::back::spv;
   155	        let mut flags = spv::WriterFlags::LABEL_VARYINGS;
   156	        flags.set(spv::WriterFlags::DEBUG, self.debug);
   157	        flags.set(
   158	            spv::WriterFlags::ADJUST_COORDINATE_SPACE,
   159	            self.adjust_coordinate_space,
   160	        );
   161	        flags.set(spv::WriterFlags::FORCE_POINT_SIZE, self.force_point_size);
   162	        flags.set(spv::WriterFlags::CLAMP_FRAG_DEPTH, self.clamp_frag_depth);
   163	        naga::back::spv::Options {
   164	            lang_version: (self.version.0, self.version.1),
   165	            flags,
   166	            capabilities: if self.capabilities.is_empty() {
   167	                None
   168	            } else {
   169	                Some(self.capabilities.clone())
   170	            },
   171	            bounds_check_policies: shared_info.bounds_checks_policies,
   172	            fake_missing_bindings: true,
   173	            binding_map: self.binding_map.clone(),
   174	            zero_initialize_workgroup_memory: spv::ZeroInitializeWorkgroupMemoryMode::Polyfill,
   175	            force_loop_bounding: true,
   176	            ray_query_initialization_tracking: true,
   177	            debug_info,
   178	            use_storage_input_output_16: self.use_storage_input_output_16,
   179	            task_dispatch_limits: shared_info.task_limits,
   180	            mesh_shader_primitive_indices_clamp: shared_info.mesh_output_validation,
   181	        }
   182	    }
   183	}
   184	
   185	#[derive(Default, serde::Deserialize)]
   186	#[serde(default)]
   187	pub struct WgslOutParameters {
   188	    pub explicit_types: bool,
   189	}
   190	impl From<&WgslOutParameters> for naga::back::wgsl::WriterFlags {
   191	    fn from(value: &WgslOutParameters) -> Self {
   192	        let mut flags = Self::empty();
   193	        flags.set(Self::EXPLICIT_TYPES, value.explicit_types);
   194	        flags
   195	    }
   196	}

   198	#[derive(Default, serde::Deserialize)]
   199	pub struct FragmentModule {
   200	    pub path: String,
   201	    pub entry_point: String,
   202	}
   203	
   204	#[derive(Default, serde::Deserialize)]
   205	#[serde(default)]
   206	pub struct Parameters {
   207	    // -- validation options --
   208	    //
   209	    // Capabilities to enable. Defaults to `Capabilities::default()`.
   210	    pub capabilities: Option<naga::valid::Capabilities>,
   211	
   212	    // -- wgsl-in options --
   213	    #[serde(rename = "wgsl-in")]
   214	    pub wgsl_in: WgslInParameters,
   215	
   216	    // -- spirv-in options --
   217	    #[serde(rename = "spv-in")]
   218	    pub spv_in: SpirvInParameters,
   219	
   220	    // -- SPIR-V options --
   221	    pub spv: SpirvOutParameters,
   222	
   223	    /// Defaults to [`Targets::non_wgsl_default()`] for `spv` and `glsl` snapshots,
   224	    /// and [`Targets::wgsl_default()`] for `wgsl` snapshots.
   225	    pub targets: Option<Targets>,
   226	
   227	    // -- MSL options --
   228	    pub msl: naga::back::msl::Options,
   229	    #[serde(default)]
   230	    pub msl_pipeline: naga::back::msl::PipelineOptions,
   231	
   232	    // -- GLSL options --
   233	    pub glsl: naga::back::glsl::Options,
   234	    pub glsl_exclude_list: naga::FastHashSet<String>,
   235	    pub glsl_multiview: Option<core::num::NonZeroU32>,
   236	
   237	    // -- HLSL options --
   238	    pub hlsl: naga::back::hlsl::Options,
   239	
   240	    // -- WGSL options --
   241	    pub wgsl: WgslOutParameters,
   242	
   243	    // -- General options --
   244	
   245	    // Allow backends to be aware of the fragment module.
   246	    // Is the name of a WGSL file in the same directory as the test file.
   247	    pub fragment_module: Option<FragmentModule>,
   248	
   249	    pub bounds_check_policies: naga::proc::BoundsCheckPolicies,
   250	    pub pipeline_constants: naga::back::PipelineConstants,
   251	
   252	    pub mesh_output_validation: bool,
   253	    #[serde(default = "default_task_limits")]
   254	    pub task_limits: Option<naga::back::TaskDispatchLimits>,
   255	}
   256	
   257	fn default_task_limits() -> Option<naga::back::TaskDispatchLimits> {
   258	    Some(naga::back::TaskDispatchLimits {
   259	        max_mesh_workgroups_per_dim: 256,
   260	        max_mesh_workgroups_total: 1024,
   261	    })
   262	}
   263	
   264	/// Information about a shader input file.
   265	#[derive(Debug)]
   266	pub struct Input {
   267	    /// The subdirectory of `tests/in` to which this input belongs, if any.
   268	    ///
   269	    /// If the subdirectory is omitted, we assume that the output goes
   270	    /// to "wgsl".
   271	    pub subdirectory: PathBuf,
   272	
   273	    /// The input filename name, without a directory.
   274	    pub file_name: PathBuf,
   275	
   276	    /// True if output filenames should add the output extension on top of
   277	    /// `file_name`'s existing extension, rather than replacing it.
   278	    ///
   279	    /// This is used by `convert_snapshots_glsl`, which wants to take input files
   280	    /// like `210-bevy-2d-shader.frag` and just add `.wgsl` to it, producing
   281	    /// `210-bevy-2d-shader.frag.wgsl`.
   282	    pub keep_input_extension: bool,
   283	}
   284	
   285	impl Input {
   286	    /// Read an input file and its corresponding parameters file.
   287	    ///
   288	    /// Given `input`, the relative path of a shader input file, return
   289	    /// a `Source` value containing its path, code, and parameters.
   290	    ///
   291	    /// The `input` path is interpreted relative to the `BASE_DIR_IN`
   292	    /// subdirectory of the directory given by the `CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR`
   293	    /// environment variable.
   294	    pub fn new(subdirectory: &str, name: &str, extension: &str) -> Input {
   295	        Input {
   296	            subdirectory: PathBuf::from(subdirectory),
   297	            // Don't wipe out any extensions on `name`, as
   298	            // `with_extension` would do.
   299	            file_name: PathBuf::from(format!("{name}.{extension}")),
   300	            keep_input_extension: false,
   301	        }
   302	    }
   303	
   304	    /// Return an iterator that produces an `Input` for each entry in `subdirectory`.
   305	    pub fn files_in_dir<'a>(
   306	        subdirectory: &'a str,
   307	        file_extensions: &'a [&'a str],
   308	        dir_in: &str,
   309	    ) -> impl Iterator<Item = Input> + 'a {
   310	        let input_directory = Path::new(dir_in).join(subdirectory);
   311	
   312	        let entries = match std::fs::read_dir(&input_directory) {
   313	            Ok(entries) => entries,
   314	            Err(err) => panic!(
   315	                "Error opening directory '{}': {}",
   316	                input_directory.display(),
   317	                err
   318	            ),
   319	        };
   320	
   321	        entries.filter_map(move |result| {
   322	            let entry = result.expect("error reading directory");
   323	            if !entry.file_type().unwrap().is_file() {
   324	                return None;
   325	            }
   326	
   327	            let file_name = PathBuf::from(entry.file_name());
   328	            let extension = file_name
   329	                .extension()
   330	                .expect("all files in snapshot input directory should have extensions");
   331	
   332	            if !file_extensions.contains(&extension.to_str().unwrap()) {
   333	                return None;
   334	            }
   335	
   336	            if let Ok(pat) = std::env::var("NAGA_SNAPSHOT") {
   337	                if !file_name.to_string_lossy().contains(&pat) {
   338	                    return None;
   339	                }
   340	            }
   341	
   342	            let input = Input::new(
   343	                subdirectory,
   344	                file_name.file_stem().unwrap().to_str().unwrap(),
   345	                extension.to_str().unwrap(),
   346	            );
   347	            Some(input)
   348	        })
   349	    }
   350	
   351	    /// Return the path to the input directory.
   352	    pub fn input_directory(&self, dir_in: &str) -> PathBuf {
   353	        Path::new(dir_in).join(&self.subdirectory)
   354	    }
   355	
   356	    /// Return the path to the output directory.
   357	    pub fn output_directory(subdirectory: &str, dir_out: &str) -> PathBuf {
   358	        Path::new(dir_out).join(subdirectory)
   359	    }
   360	
   361	    /// Return the path to the input file.
   362	    pub fn input_path(&self, dir_in: &str) -> PathBuf {
   363	        let mut input = self.input_directory(dir_in);
   364	        input.push(&self.file_name);
   365	        input
   366	    }
   367	
   368	    pub fn output_path(&self, subdirectory: &str, extension: &str, dir_out: &str) -> PathBuf {
   369	        let mut output = Self::output_directory(subdirectory, dir_out);
   370	        if self.keep_input_extension {
   371	            let file_name = format!(
   372	                "{}-{}.{}",
   373	                self.subdirectory.display(),
   374	                self.file_name.display(),
   375	                extension
   376	            );
   377	
   378	            output.push(&file_name);
   379	        } else {
   380	            let file_name = format!(
   381	                "{}-{}",
   382	                self.subdirectory.display(),
   383	                self.file_name.display()
   384	            );
   385	
   386	            output.push(&file_name);
   387	            output.set_extension(extension);
   388	        }
   389	        output
   390	    }
   391	
   392	    /// Return the contents of the input file as a string.
   393	    pub fn read_source(&self, dir_in: &str, print: bool) -> String {
   394	        if print {
   395	            println!("Processing '{}'", self.file_name.display());
   396	        }
   397	        let input_path = self.input_path(dir_in);
   398	        match fs::read_to_string(&input_path) {
   399	            Ok(source) => source,
   400	            Err(err) => {
   401	                panic!(
   402	                    "Couldn't read shader input file `{}`: {}",
   403	                    input_path.display(),
   404	                    err
   405	                );
   406	            }
   407	        }
   408	    }
   409	
   410	    /// Return the contents of the input file as a vector of bytes.
   411	    pub fn read_bytes(&self, dir_in: &str, print: bool) -> Vec<u8> {
   412	        if print {
   413	            println!("Processing '{}'", self.file_name.display());
   414	        }
   415	        let input_path = self.input_path(dir_in);
   416	        match fs::read(&input_path) {
   417	            Ok(bytes) => bytes,
   418	            Err(err) => {
   419	                panic!(
   420	                    "Couldn't read shader input file `{}`: {}",
   421	                    input_path.display(),
   422	                    err
   423	                );
   424	            }
   425	        }
   426	    }
   427	
   428	    pub fn bytes(&self, dir_in: &str) -> u64 {
   429	        let input_path = self.input_path(dir_in);
   430	        std::fs::metadata(input_path).unwrap().len()
   431	    }
   432	
   433	    /// Return this input's parameter file, parsed.
   434	    pub fn read_parameters(&self, dir_in: &str) -> Parameters {
   435	        let mut param_path = self.input_path(dir_in);
   436	        param_path.set_extension("toml");
   437	        let mut params = match fs::read_to_string(&param_path) {
   438	            Ok(string) => match toml::de::from_str(&string) {
   439	                Ok(params) => params,
   440	                Err(e) => panic!(
   441	                    "Couldn't parse param file: {} due to: {e}",
   442	                    param_path.display()
   443	                ),
   444	            },
   445	            Err(_) => Parameters::default(),
   446	        };
   447	
   448	        if params.targets.is_none() {
   449	            match self
   450	                .input_path(dir_in)
   451	                .extension()
   452	                .unwrap()
   453	                .to_str()
   454	                .unwrap()
   455	            {
   456	                "wgsl" => params.targets = Some(Targets::wgsl_default()),
   457	                "spvasm" => params.targets = Some(Targets::non_wgsl_default()),
   458	                "vert" | "frag" | "comp" => params.targets = Some(Targets::non_wgsl_default()),
   459	                e => {
   460	                    panic!("Unknown extension: {e}");
   461	                }
   462	            }
   463	        }
   464	
   465	        params
   466	    }
   467	
   468	    /// Write `data` to a file corresponding to this input file in
   469	    /// `subdirectory`, with `extension`.
   470	    pub fn write_output_file(
   471	        &self,
   472	        subdirectory: &str,
   473	        extension: &str,
   474	        data: impl AsRef<[u8]>,
   475	        dir_out: &str,
   476	    ) {
   477	        let output_path = self.output_path(subdirectory, extension, dir_out);
   478	        fs::create_dir_all(output_path.parent().unwrap()).unwrap();
   479	        if let Err(err) = fs::write(&output_path, data) {
   480	            panic!("Error writing {}: {}", output_path.display(), err);
   481	        }
   482	    }
   483	}
   484

Good, reverted. Now let me also think about edge cases:

1. `@must_use` on `fn` with no return type - already handled by `FunctionMustUseReturnsVoid`
2. `@must_use` on `fn` with return type - works correctly (passed to function_decl)
3. `@must_use` on `var` - now returns `UnknownAttribute`
4. `@must_use` on `struct` - now returns `UnknownAttribute`
5. `@must_use` on `const` - now returns `UnknownAttribute`
6. `@must_use` on `override` - now returns `UnknownAttribute`
7. `@must_use` on `alias` - now returns `UnknownAttribute`
8. `@must_use` on `const_assert` - now returns `UnknownAttribute`

Wait, I should also think about the case where there's just an `@must_use` attribute with no declaration (just `@must_use;`). Let me check that case:

Looking at line 2052-2058:
```
(Token::Separator(';'), _) => {
    ensure_no_diag_attrs(
        DiagnosticAttributeNotSupportedPosition::SemicolonInModulePosition,
        diagnostic_filters,
    )?;
    None
}
```

This handles the case where there's just a `;` after attributes (no declaration). In that case `kind` is `None`, so my check won't apply. But the `must_use` value is just silently ignored. That might be another issue, but the PR description specifically mentions `var` declarations, so the current fix should be sufficient.

Also, I should double-check that the repeated `must_use` attribute on non-functions also produces the right error. Currently, the `ParsedAttribute::set` method returns `RepeatedAttribute` on duplicate. But my check runs AFTER the attribute loop. If someone writes `@must_use @must_use var x;`, the second `@must_use` would trigger `RepeatedAttribute` error first (since `set` is called before we even look at the declaration kind). That's the correct behavior - the repeated attribute error should take precedence.

Let me also verify that the fix doesn't break the case where `@must_use` is used on a function. Let me run the existing must_use tests again:

Let me check if there's a way to verify my change independently. Let me look at the PR description more carefully:

The PR says:
- `@must_use @group(0) @binding(0) var<storage> x : array<u32>;` should produce a validation error
- Currently it doesn't produce an error

My fix makes it produce `UnknownAttribute` error for `@must_use` on non-function global declarations.

Let me also think about whether `UnknownAttribute` is the right error. Looking at the `struct_member_must_use` test, it uses `UnknownAttribute`. And for consistency, my fix uses the same error. The PR description says "Expected validation error" - `UnknownAttribute` should be fine.

But wait, I need to also consider: should `@must_use` on a function be valid even if the function doesn't return anything? That's already handled by `FunctionMustUseReturnsVoid`. And `@must_use` on a function with a return type works correctly.

Let me also double-check the edge case where someone puts `@must_use` on a `const_assert`. My fix covers that through the `!matches!(kind, ast::GlobalDeclKind::Fn(_))` check.

Now, I should also consider: what about `@must_use` on a declaration with just `;` (empty declaration)? Looking at lines 2052-2058, when the token after attributes is `;`, `kind` is `None`. In that case, my check at line 2206 won't execute (since `kind` is `None`). The `must_use` attribute would be silently ignored. But this is probably fine because an empty declaration with just `@must_use;` doesn't make much sense anyway, and there are no attributes being used on nothing.

Actually, wait - if someone writes `@must_use;`, the attribute is parsed but then `kind` is `None`, so nothing happens. But this is the same for all other attributes too (like `@compute;` would also be silently ignored). So this is consistent.

2026-06-01 04:09:04,895 - INFO - swea-agent-gfx-rs__wgpu-9367 - 💭 THOUGHT
The fix looks good. Let me now think about whether there are any other edge cases I might have missed:

1. What if there's a `let` declaration at module scope? That's not possible - module level only has `var`, `fn`, `struct`, `const`, `alias`, `override`, `const_assert`. All covered.

2. What about diagnostic filters? The `ensure_no_diag_attrs` calls are still made for each non-function kind, and those are separate from the `must_use` check.

3. What about the case where `must_use` appears with other attributes? The attribute parsing loop handles all attributes, and the `must_use` is just collected. Then after the kind is determined, we check if `must_use` is set on a non-function.

I believe my fix is complete and correct.

Let me also check if there's a need to handle the case where `@must_use` is on something that starts with `;` (empty declaration). Looking at the code flow:

- If the token after attributes is `;`, `kind` is `None` (line 2057), and the `if let Some(kind) = kind` block at line 2206 is skipped. So `must_use` would be silently ignored. But this is the same behavior for all other attributes (like `@compute;` would also be silently ignored). This seems intentional - the semicolon handling is for empty declarations.

Actually, wait. Let me look at what happens for `@compute;` - it has `stage` set but the declaration is empty. Looking at lines 2052-2058:

```rust
(Token::Separator(';'), _) => {
    ensure_no_diag_attrs(
        DiagnosticAttributeNotSupportedPosition::SemicolonInModulePosition,
        diagnostic_filters,
    )?;
    None
}
```

This just ensures no diagnostic attributes and returns `None`, effectively ignoring all other attributes. So `@must_use;` would be silently ignored, which is consistent. But should `@must_use;` also be an error? That seems like a separate issue from what the PR describes. The PR specifically talks about `var` declarations.

I think my fix is sufficient for the PR requirements.

Let me also double-check by reading the PR description one more time:

> naga does not validate `must_use` for global declarations
> ```
> [fail] webgpu:shader,validation,parse,must_use:declaration:test="var"
> @must_use @group(0) @binding(0)
>     var<storage> x : array<u32>;
> ```

The fix ensures that `@must_use` on a `var` (or any non-function global declaration) produces an `UnknownAttribute` error.
